text: The surgeon performs a therapeutic lymphadenectomy of the presacral basin, located between the common iliac arteries, sacral promontory, and hypogastric nerves. The inferior hypogastric nerves are lifted to liberate fibrofatty tissue beneath them. The periosteum of the sacral promontory is visible, and fibrofatty tissue is dissected using laparoscopic shearer and Bissinger bipolar instruments. The left common iliac vein is carefully dissected to avoid damage.

The surgeon carefully lifts and dissects fibro-fatty tissue from the sacral promontory using tactile sensation, focusing on coagulating feeder vessels to maintain a bloodless field. The assistant provides traction, and the bipolar device is used to liberate a large chunk of tissue from the presacral basin for histopathological examination.

The surgeon focuses on the common iliac lymphadenectomy on the right side, clearing the lymphatic basin located under the venous channels. Using a laparoscopic shearer through the middle port, the dissection delineates the surgical plane, exposing the inferior vena cava and its perforator vessels. Non-graspable traction with a needle holder maneuvers the ureter from the mesourator, exposing the common iliac basin. Care is taken to avoid hemorrhage from the perforators of the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon begins the procedure by exposing the psoas muscle, revealing the fibro fatty tissue beneath the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the right common iliac vein. This tissue is carefully dissected and removed for histopathological examination as part of the therapeutic lymph node clearance. The surgeon spares the nerves while dissecting the fibro fatty tissue, staying close to the vein to facilitate mobilization. Perforating vessels are coagulated safely during the dissection of lymph nodes beneath the common iliac and the beginning of the IVC. The dissection proceeds from lateral to medial, moving from venous to arterial channels towards the presacral basin, ensuring thorough clearance. The inter arteriovenous lymphatic channels are meticulously separated to liberate the lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the lymphatic tissue package, sending it for histopathological examination. The left common iliac vein is exposed and de-roofed, revealing the underlying structures. Using the Bissinger bipolar and laparoscopic shearer, the fibro fatty tissue is separated and coagulated as needed. The left common iliac vein is visible beneath the right common iliac artery, and the entire lymphatic tissue is removed as a single package. The surgical field remains bloodless, with minimal suctioning required to clear small fluid accumulations. The left common iliac basin is prepared by exposing the psoas muscle and identifying surrounding anatomical structures, including the ureter, left common iliac artery, and hypogastric plexus.

The surgeon gently dissects the para-aortic basin, exposing the inferior mesenteric artery forming the roof. The psoas muscle and ureter are clearly visible, with the fibro fatty tissue being compromised for the left common iliac lymphadenectomy. The assistant uses an atraumatic needle holder for firm traction, exposing the left common iliac artery and preparing the lymphatic package for liberation. Care is taken around perforator vessels, with suction applied to maintain a clear field.

The surgeon carefully liberates the lymphatic tissue package from the surrounding structures, using bipolar coagulation to manage small perforator vessels that cause minor bleeding. The tissue is sticky and tougher as the dissection progresses upwards. The package is gently handled to avoid stretching or pulling, ensuring it is intact for histopathological examination. The surgical field is cleared of fibro-fatty tissue, completing the therapeutic common iliac lymphadenectomy.
